content:
<USER_REQUEST>
Write 7 detailed skill documentation files for future AI agents and human developers. These go in `d:\portfolio-draft\docs\skills\`. Each should be comprehensive with code examples, gotchas, and lessons learned.

## File 1: `d:\portfolio-draft\docs\skills\hermes-skills.md`
Title: "Writing Hermes Agent Skills"
Cover:
- SKILL.md format: YAML frontmatter (name, description, version, author, license, platforms, metadata.hermes.tags/related_skills/requires_tools) + markdown body
- Directory structure: SKILL.md + optional scripts/, references/, templates/
- Skills live in `~/.hermes/skills/` or external dirs via config.yaml `skills.external_dirs`
- CLI: `hermes skills search`, `hermes skills install`, `/skills browse`
- Skill vs Tool: Skills = instructions + shell commands (no custom Python). Tools = custom Python logic
- Hermes v0.16.0 specifics: 70+ built-in tools, dashboard on :9119, gateway setup for Telegram
- Config: `~/.hermes/config.yaml` + `~/.hermes/.env`, precedence: CLI > config > .env > defaults
- Memory: MEMORY.md (2,200 chars) + USER.md (1,375 chars) injected into system prompt
- Key gotcha: Requires model with ≥64,000 token context window
- Key gotcha: WSL2 needs systemd=true in /etc/wsl.conf and metadata mount options

## File 2: `d:\portfolio-draft\docs\skills\model-routing.md`
Title: "LLM Model Routing Architecture"
Cover:
- The 3-layer pattern: Rules (0ms) → Semantic Router (5-20ms) → LLM Classifier (100-300ms)
- Why layered: 95% of traffic handled without LLM call
- Libraries: LiteLLM (proxy + failover), semantic-router (embedding intent), RouteLLM (ML classifiers)
- LiteLLM config.yaml format: model_list, router_settings, fallbacks, cache
- semantic-router: Route objects with utterances, HuggingFaceEncoder for local
- Privacy override: always checked post-classification, force local on sensitive patterns
- Logging: SQLite for routing decisions, track distribution/latency/cost
- Config pattern: layered YAML (defaults → user config → env vars)
- Gotcha: semantic-router encod
<truncated 2920 bytes>
iers: Marquee ≥₹20 LPA, Super Dream ≥₹10 LPA
- Joel's competitive edge: published research + 4 certs + 8 languages + creative portfolio
- Top 3 gaps: cloud cert (AWS), RAG project, internship experience
- LinkedIn formula: "[Role] | [Stack] | [Value Prop] | [Institution]"
- GitHub optimization: profile README, 6 pinned repos with READMEs + GIFs
- Blogging strategy: publish on Hashnode (custom domain) → syndicate to Dev.to
- Hackathon strategy: target hiring hackathons (Goldman Sachs, Flipkart GRID, SIH)
- Cold email: signal-based, <120 words, reference specific work, ask for 15-min call
- Content that works: deep-dive tutorials, "fixing" posts, case studies
- Indian AI boom: $1.48B funded Q1 2026, Sarvam AI at $1.5B
- Cert ROI: AWS Cloud Practitioner ($100, 2 weeks, +₹1-2 LPA)
- Files to maintain: docs/personal/database.md, career_analytics.md, market_insights.md, marketing_strategy.md, self_improvement_plan.md, llm_context.md

## File 7: `d:\portfolio-draft\docs\skills\project-handoffs.md`
Title: "Writing Agent Handoff Documents"
Cover:
- Always save handoff in 3 places: artifact dir, project root (AGENT_HANDOFF.md), related projects
- Structure: User Profile → System Specs → Project Status → File Inventory → What's Done → What's Pending → How to Continue → Architecture Quick Reference → Open Questions → Design Decisions
- Include exact file paths with sizes
- Include exact subagent prompts for re-running failed work
- Include config schemas inline (don't reference external files only)
- Include port assignments and service mappings
- Include credentials needed (as placeholder names, never actual values)
- Always note which agents hit errors and why
- Note conversation ID for transcript access
- Gotcha: Keep handoff <15 KB — next agent's context is limited
- Gotcha: Always check actual file state before writing handoff (agents may have written more/less than reported)
</USER_REQUEST>

args.CodeContent: "# Writing Hermes Agent Skills > A comprehensive guide for AI agents and human developers building, installing, and managing skills for the Hermes AI assistant (v0.16.0+). --- ## Table of Contents 1. [What Are Skills?](#what-are-skills) 2. [Skill vs Tool — When to Use Each](#skill-vs-tool) 3. [SKILL.md Format](#skillmd-format) 4. [Directory Structure](#directory-structure) 5. [Skill Location & Discovery](#skill-location--discovery) 6. [CLI Commands](#cli-commands) 7. [Hermes v0.16.0 Architecture](#hermes-v0160-architecture) 8. [Configuration Deep-Dive](#configuration-deep-dive) 9. [Memory System](#memory-system) 10. [Gotchas & Lessons Learned](#gotchas--lessons-learned) 11. [Complete Example: A "Git Commit Helper" Skill](#complete-example) 12. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) --- ## 1. What Are Skills? Skills are **instruction documents** that extend Hermes's capabilities without writing custom Python code. Think of them as "playbooks" — they tell the agent *how* to accomplish a task using its existing tools (shell commands, file I/O, browser, etc.). A skill is activated when Hermes detects that a user's request matches the skill's domain. The skill's instructions are then injected into the agent's context, guiding its behavior. **Key properties:** - Skills are **declarative** — they describe *what* to do, not *how* to implement it - Skills use **existing tools** — shell commands, file operations, built-in tools - Skills are **portable** — just markdown files with optional scripts - Skills are **discoverable** — searchable via CLI and the `/skills browse` command --- ## 2. Skill vs Tool — When to Use Each This is the most common source of confusion. Here's the definitive guide: | Aspect | Skill | Tool | |--------|-------|------| | **Format** | Markdown (SKILL.md) + optional shell scripts | Custom Python module | | **Complexity** | Instructions + shell commands | Arbitrary P
args.Description: "Comprehensive documentation on writing Hermes agent skills — covers SKILL.md format, directory structure, CLI, config, memory system, and WSL2 gotchas."

args.CodeContent: "# LLM Model Routing Architecture > A comprehensive guide to building intelligent, cost-effective model routing systems that direct requests to the right LLM based on complexity, privacy, and cost constraints. --- ## Table of Contents 1. [Why Model Routing?](#why-model-routing) 2. [The 3-Layer Pattern](#the-3-layer-pattern) 3. [Layer 1: Rule-Based Router (0ms)](#layer-1-rule-based-router) 4. [Layer 2: Semantic Router (5-20ms)](#layer-2-semantic-router) 5. [Layer 3: LLM Classifier (100-300ms)](#layer-3-llm-classifier) 6. [Privacy Override System](#privacy-override-system) 7. [LiteLLM Proxy Configuration](#litellm-proxy-configuration) 8. [semantic-router Library](#semantic-router-library) 9. [RouteLLM Integration](#routellm-integration) 10. [Logging & Observability](#logging--observability) 11. [Config Pattern: Layered YAML](#config-pattern-layered-yaml) 12. [Cost Analysis](#cost-analysis) 13. [Gotchas & Lessons Learned](#gotchas--lessons-learned) 14. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) --- ## 1. Why Model Routing? Not every request needs GPT-4o or Claude Sonnet 4. A "what time is it?" question doesn't need a $0.015/1K-token model — a $0.0001/1K-token model works fine. **Model routing** automatically classifies incoming requests and routes them to the most cost-effective model that can handle the task. ### The Economics | Request Type | % of Traffic | Best Model | Cost/1K tokens | |-------------|-------------|------------|----------------| | Simple Q&A | ~40% | Llama 3.3 8B (local) | $0.00 | | Code generation | ~25% | Claude Sonnet 4 | $0.003 | | Complex reasoning | ~10% | o3 / Gemini 2.5 Pro | $0.015 | | Creative writing | ~15% | GPT-4o | $0.005 | | Sensitive/private | ~10% | Local model (forced) | $0.00 | **Without routing:** Everything goes to a premium model → ~$0.008/avg per 1K tokens **With routing:** Tiered models → ~$0.002/avg per 1K tokens → **75% savings** --- ## 2. The 3-Layer Pattern
args.Description: "Comprehensive guide to LLM model routing — covers the 3-layer pattern (rules/semantic/LLM), LiteLLM proxy config, semantic-router, RouteLLM, privacy overrides, and cost analysis."

args.CodeContent: "# WSL2 Automation & Installer Scripts > A comprehensive guide to automating WSL2 setup, service management, Docker/GPU configuration, and idempotent installer patterns for AI workstations. --- ## Table of Contents 1. [Creating Named WSL Distros](#creating-named-wsl-distros) 2. [Managing WSL Instances](#managing-wsl-instances) 3. [/etc/wsl.conf — Complete Reference](#wslconf-reference) 4. [Installing Docker Engine (Without Docker Desktop)](#docker-engine) 5. [NVIDIA Container Toolkit for GPU Passthrough](#nvidia-gpu) 6. [systemd Services in WSL2](#systemd-services) 7. [Creating systemd User Services](#user-services) 8. [Idempotent Installer Pattern](#idempotent-installer) 9. [Port Forwarding & Networking](#port-forwarding) 10. [Gotchas & Lessons Learned](#gotchas) 11. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) --- ## 1. Creating Named WSL Distros WSL2 supports multiple named instances of the same distribution. This is essential for isolating environments (e.g., one for AI workloads, one for web dev). ### Install a Named Distro ```powershell # List available distros wsl --list --online # Install with a custom name wsl --install Ubuntu-24.04 --name agentos # Or with Ubuntu 26.04 (if available) wsl --install Ubuntu-26.04 --name agentos ``` ### Full Setup Workflow ```powershell # Step 1: Install the distro wsl --install Ubuntu-24.04 --name agentos # Step 2: Set as default (optional) wsl --set-default agentos # Step 3: Enter the distro wsl -d agentos # Step 4: Set up your user (first run prompts for this) # Username: joel # Password: (your choice) # Step 5: Update packages sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y ``` ### Import/Export for Backup ```powershell # Export a distro to a tar file (backup) wsl --export agentos D:\backups\agentos-backup.tar # Import a distro from a tar file (restore) wsl --import agentos-restored D:\wsl\agentos-restored D:\backups\agentos-backup.tar # This create
args.Description: "Comprehensive WSL2 automation guide — covers distro management, Docker Engine, NVIDIA GPU, systemd services, idempotent installers, networking, and common encoding/config gotchas."

args.CodeContent: "# Building n8n Workflows > A comprehensive guide for AI agents and developers building, configuring, and deploying n8n automation workflows — including AI agent nodes, email automation, and self-hosted setup. --- ## Table of Contents 1. [What is n8n?](#what-is-n8n) 2. [Installation & Setup](#installation) 3. [n8n JSON Format](#json-format) 4. [Common Nodes Reference](#common-nodes) 5. [Credential Management](#credentials) 6. [AI Agent Node Configuration](#ai-agent-node) 7. [Best Practices & Patterns](#best-practices) 8. [Self-Hosted Configuration](#self-hosted) 9. [Complete Workflow Example](#complete-example) 10. [Gotchas & Lessons Learned](#gotchas) 11. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) --- ## 1. What is n8n? n8n is an open-source workflow automation tool — think Zapier but self-hosted, with a code-capable function node, and an AI agent node that connects to any OpenAI-compatible API. **Key advantages for developers:** - **Self-hosted** — Your data stays on your infrastructure - **Code nodes** — Write JavaScript/Python when visual nodes aren't enough - **AI Agent node** — Connect to LiteLLM, Ollama, or any OpenAI-compatible endpoint - **400+ integrations** — Gmail, Slack, Telegram, Google Sheets, GitHub, etc. - **Webhook triggers** — Any external service can trigger a workflow --- ## 2. Installation & Setup ### Quick Install (npm) ```bash # Install globally npm install -g n8n # Start n8n (default port 5678) n8n start # Or start with specific settings N8N_PORT=5678 \ N8N_PROTOCOL=http \ GENERIC_TIMEZONE=Asia/Kolkata \ n8n start ``` ### Docker Install ```bash docker run -d \ --name n8n \ -p 5678:5678 \ -v n8n_data:/home/node/.n8n \ -e GENERIC_TIMEZONE=Asia/Kolkata \ -e N8N_SECURE_COOKIE=false \ n8nio/n8n ``` ### Access the UI Open `http://localhost:5678` in your browser. On first launch, you'll create an admin account. ### Environm
args.Description: "Comprehensive n8n workflow guide — covers JSON format, common nodes (Gmail, Sheets, Telegram, AI Agent), credential management, self-hosted setup, and practical gotchas about IMAP passwords and draft patterns."

args.CodeContent: "# Astro + React Islands Architecture > A comprehensive guide for building Astro 5.x sites with React islands, xterm.js integration, 3D CSS transforms, CRT effects, and the ThinkPad-themed portfolio architecture. --- ## Table of Contents 1. [Astro 5.x Mental Model](#astro-mental-model) 2. [Islands Architecture & Partial Hydration](#islands-architecture) 3. [React Islands — Client Directives](#react-islands) 4. [xterm.js Integration](#xtermjs-integration) 5. [CSS Organization & Design Tokens](#css-organization) 6. [3D CSS Transforms — Laptop Lid Animation](#3d-transforms) 7. [ThinkPad Aesthetic — CSS Deep Dive](#thinkpad-aesthetic) 8. [Easter Egg System](#easter-egg-system) 9. [Portfolio Architecture: LaptopShell → Terminal](#portfolio-architecture) 10. [Development Workflow](#dev-workflow) 11. [Gotchas & Lessons Learned](#gotchas) 12. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) --- ## 1. Astro 5.x Mental Model Astro is a **static-first** framework. The default behavior is to ship **zero JavaScript** to the browser. Components render to HTML at build time, and only the parts you explicitly mark as interactive ("islands") get hydrated with JavaScript. ### Core Principles 1. **Server-first**: Components run on the server by default. No JS shipped unless you opt in. 2. **Islands**: Interactive components are "islands" of JavaScript in a "sea" of static HTML. 3. **Framework-agnostic**: Use React, Vue, Svelte, or Solid — even mix them on the same page. 4. **Partial hydration**: Only load JS for the components that need it, when they need it. ### How It Differs from Next.js/Vite | Feature | Astro | Next.js | Vite (React) | |---------|-------|---------|-------------| | Default JS | Zero | Full bundle | Full bundle | | Rendering | Static HTML | SSR + Hydration | CSR | | Interactivity | Opt-in (islands) | Opt-out (`use server`) | Always on | | Routing | File-based | File-based | Manual (react-router) | | Best for | Content site
args.Description: "Comprehensive guide to Astro + React islands architecture — covers partial hydration, xterm.js integration, 3D CSS laptop animation, ThinkPad aesthetic, easter egg system, CRT effects, and the portfolio component hierarchy."

[VIT Chennai Placement Tiers](#placement-tiers) 2. [Competitive Positioning Analysis](#competitive-positioning) 3. [Gap Analysis & Action Plans](#gap-analysis) 4. [LinkedIn Optimization](#linkedin) 5. [GitHub Profile Engineering](#github) 6. [Blogging & Content Strategy](#blogging) 7. [Hackathon Strategy](#hackathons) 8. [Cold Email Templates](#cold-emails) 9. [Content Formats That Work](#content-formats) 10. [Indian AI Market Landscape (2026)](#ai-market) 11. [Certification ROI Analysis](#cert-roi) 12. [Files to Maintain](#files-to-maintain) 13. [Monthly Execution Checklist](#monthly-checklist) 14. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) --- ## 1. VIT Chennai Placement Tiers VIT Chennai follows a structured placement system with distinct tiers: | Tier | Package (LPA) | Typical Companies | Seats | Selection Rate | |------|--------------|-------------------|-------|---------------| | **Marquee** | ≥₹20 LPA | Google, Microsoft, Goldman Sachs, Tower Research, DE Shaw | 5-15 | ~2% | | **Super Dream** | ≥₹10 LPA | Amazon, Flipkart, Walmart, Intuit, SAP Labs, Adobe | 30-60 | ~8% | | **Dream** | ≥₹6 LPA | TCS Digital, Infosys Power Programmer, Cognizant GenC Pro | 100-200 | ~20% | | **Regular** | ₹3-6 LPA | Wipro, Infosys, TCS, Capgemini, HCL | 400+ | ~60% | ### What Each Tier Looks For **Marquee (₹20+ LPA):** - Strong DSA (LeetCode hard in 30 min) - System design fundamentals - Research/publications (huge differentiator) - Open-source contributions - Unique projects (not TODO apps) **Super Dream (₹10-20 LPA):** - Solid DSA (LeetCode medium consistently) - Good project portfolio (3-4 meaningful projects) - Internship experience - Clear communication
args.Description: "Comprehensive developer personal branding playbook for Indian CS students — covers VIT placement tiers, competitive positioning, LinkedIn/GitHub optimization, blogging, hackathon strategy, cold email templates, and certification ROI analysis."

[Why Handoffs Matter](#why-handoffs-matter) 2. [The 3-Place Rule](#three-place-rule) 3. [Document Structure — Complete Reference](#document-structure) 4. [Complete Handoff Template](#complete-template) 5. [Writing Each Section](#writing-each-section) 6. [File Paths & Inventories](#file-inventories) 7. [Subagent Prompt Documentation](#subagent-prompts) 8. [Inline Config Schemas](#inline-configs) 9. [Port Assignments & Service Maps](#port-assignments) 10. [Credential Placeholders](#credential-placeholders) 11. [Error Tracking](#error-tracking) 12. [Conversation ID & Transcript Access](#conversation-ids) 13. [Gotchas & Lessons Learned](#gotchas) 14. [Pre-Handoff Verification Checklist](#verification-checklist) 15. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) --- ## 1. Why Handoffs Matter AI agents have **limited context windows** and **no persistent memory** between sessions. Without a handoff document: - The next agent starts from scratch, re-reading the entire codebase - Design decisions are lost — the next agent might contradict them - Partially completed work is abandoned or duplicated - Time is wasted re-discovering project state A good handoff document is the difference between a 5-minute continuation and a 2-hour re-onboarding. ### Who Reads Handoffs? 1. **Future AI agents** — May be the same model or a different one. They have limited context, so conciseness matters. 2. **Human developers** — May need to understand what was built, why, and what's left. 3. **You (the writing agent)** — If resumed in the same conversation, you may need to re-read your own handoff. ### The Core Principle > **Write the document you wish you had received.** If you were a new agent dropp
